# Figure description: скв_157.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/drill_holes_data/скв_157.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite borehole/well log (геологический разрез скважины / каротажная диаграмма)** — a multi-column well log typical of Soviet-era drilling documentation. It combines a lithological column with multiple geophysical wire-line log curves and a tabular data section, presented on a continuous vertical depth strip. The format is consistent with standard Soviet бурение (drilling) log sheets used in regional 1:200,000 geological surveys.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log appears to cover a **single deep borehole** (скважина), likely one of the parametric or structural drilling holes associated with the regional survey of map sheets **M-42 and/or N-42** in northern Kazakhstan. The depth range appears to be substantial — the log runs the full height of a very long folded sheet, suggesting a depth of likely **several hundred to over 1,000 metres**. The specific borehole number (e.g., скв-[number]) is present in the header but is **[illegible]** at this scan resolution. The header region at the top contains the borehole identification, location data, and column headings.

---

## Key Content

The log is organised into several vertical columns running from top to bottom, representing increasing depth. The following column types are identifiable:

### Left Side — Lithological and Structural Columns:
- **Depth scale column** (leftmost): Marked in metres, running continuously from top to bottom. Depth increments appear to be every 10 or 20 metres, with major divisions possibly every 100 m.
- **Lithological pattern column**: Shows a continuous sequence of rock types rendered using standard Soviet lithological symbols (stippling for sandstone/sandy layers, brick-pattern for limestone/carbonate, dashes or horizontal lines for claystone/mudstone, dotted patterns for conglomerate or coarse clastic material). Several distinct **lithological units** are visible:
  - Upper section: appears dominated by fine-grained or argillaceous sediments (close horizontal hatching)
  - Middle sections: alternating coarser and finer units, with some intervals showing carbonate or harder rock patterns
  - Lower sections: possibly crystalline basement or dense rock (more uniform fill pattern)
- **Stratigraphic/formation column**: A narrow column adjacent to lithology likely indicating formation or stratigraphic unit boundaries (Свита / Formation names), with interval brackets. Specific formation names are **[illegible]**.
- **Core recovery or sample column**: Possibly present as a narrow column noting intervals of core recovery.

### Centre — Geophysical Log Curves:
Two or three **wire-line geophysical curves** are plotted as continuous wiggle traces against depth. These are characteristic of Soviet КС (кажущееся сопротивление — apparent resistivity), ПС (потенциал самополяризации — spontaneous potential/SP), and possibly ГК (гамма-каротаж — gamma ray) or НГК (нейтронный гамма-каротаж — neutron-gamma log):

- **Left curve (likely SP/ПС)**: Shows moderate amplitude deflections, with notable anomalies at several depth intervals suggesting lithological contrasts (permeable vs. impermeable beds).
- **Central/right curve (likely resistivity/КС)**: Shows higher amplitude, sharper deflections — pronounced high-resistivity spikes likely corresponding to carbonates, dense sandstones, or igneous/metamorphic intervals. Several very prominent high-amplitude excursions are visible in the middle-to-lower portion of the log, suggesting **resistive layers of potential interest** (possibly mineralized zones, carbonates, or basement rocks).
- A third, narrower curve may represent **gamma ray or caliper**, showing less dramatic variation.

The curves show several **zones of strong anomaly** (high deflection) that would correlate with lithological boundaries or potentially mineralised intervals — these are the most visually prominent features of the log.

### Right Side — Tabular Data Section:
A multi-column **numerical data table** occupies the right approximately one-third of the sheet. This is a standard Soviet well log data table and likely contains columns for:
- Depth intervals (От/До — From/To)
- Rock description or lithological code
- Sample/core numbers
- Geophysical log values at specific depths
- Possibly mineralogical or chemical assay data

The columns appear to have **printed headers** at the top (now largely **[illegible]**) and rows of numerical and letter entries running down the full depth of the log. Some entries in this table appear to contain single-digit numbers and letters, possibly porosity codes, formation codes, or sample identifiers.

## Text and Labels

Due to the very low resolution and faded nature of the scan, most text is **[illegible]**. The following structural text elements are identifiable:

**Header (top of sheet):**
- A title block with multiple lines — likely contains: бorehole number, coordinates, location name, survey organisation, year, and map sheet reference. Partially readable fragments suggest standard Soviet бурение header format. Specific values **[illegible]**.
- Column headers visible across top — appear to include abbreviations consistent with: **Глубина** (Depth), **Литология** (Lithology), **ПС** (SP log), **КС** (Resistivity), and tabular column headers on right side.

**Along depth scale (left margin):**
- Depth values in metres are present at regular intervals. Some legible values appear to include round numbers suggesting increments of **10 m** with major gridlines at **100 m intervals**. Specific values **[illegible]** at this resolution.

**Formation/stratigraphic labels (narrow column):**
- Several bracketed intervals with Cyrillic text — likely Свита (suite/formation) names or age designations (e.g., possibly **P**, **K**, **J**, **C**, **D** for Palaeogene, Cretaceous, Jurassic, Carboniferous, Devonian, etc.) — **[illegible]**.

**Annotations on curves:**
- Some numerical scale values appear along the top of the curve columns (curve scales in ohm-metres for КС, mV for ПС) — **[illegible]**.

**Bottom of sheet:**
- A small legend/key box appears near the lower right — likely explaining lithological symbols used in the log. Content **[illegible]**.
